data.title: Virtu Financial stock climbs after strong Q4 earnings beat
data.description:
Virtu Financial (VIRT) jumped 6.8% in Thursday afternoon trading after the market-making firm delivered Q4 2025 earnings that easily exceeded Wall Street expectations on higher trading income.

Q4 normalized adjusted EPS of $1.85, well above the $1.15 expected, advanced from $1.14 in the year-ago quarter.

Net trading income increased 22.2% to $664.9M Y/Y in Q4, compared with $544.0M in Q4 2024.

Market Making net trading income totaled $657.0M in the three months ended Dec. 31, 2025, up from $534.7M in 2024's Q4.

Execution Services commissions, net and technology services of $148.0M in Q4 vs. $127.3M a year before.

Total interest and dividends income rose to $143.9M from $123.8M in Q4 2024.

Total operating expenses were $626.3M in Q4, down from $631.7M a year ago.

data.title: MarineMax reaffirms $110M–$125M adjusted EBITDA guidance for 2026 as inventory and premium sales trends improve
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MarineMax (HZO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Bill McGill acknowledged ongoing market challenges, noting, "Market conditions remained challenging throughout the quarter, with elevated promotional activity and cautious retail behavior continuing to influence demand patterns." He highlighted that despite these conditions, the company delivered year-over-year revenue growth supported by strong same-store sales and a focus on premium brand offerings and larger products.
  • McGill emphasized inventory management, stating the team reduced inventory by nearly $170 million year-over-year and expects "the industry's inventory environment to continue progressing toward more normalized levels as we move into the second half of the fiscal year."
  • Strategic focus remains on expanding higher margin, less cyclical businesses such as Marinas, Storage, Service, and Finance and Insurance, which are "elevating our performance and will enhance our cash flows as the retail boating industry begins to recover."
  • CFO Michael McLamb stated, "Looking at our first quarter results, achieving nearly 11% same-store sales growth was encouraging. Our revenue of $505 million supported our efforts to reduce inventory and contributed to an even stronger, more liquid balance sheet."
  • McLamb also noted, "Interest expense also declined due to decreased borrowings from lower inventory and lower rates. Interest expense should continue to be a tailwind for us in fiscal 2026 compared with last year."

Outlook

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 is reaffirmed, with adjusted EBITDA expected in the range of $110 million to $125 million and adjusted net income between $0.40 and $0.95 per diluted share.
  • McLamb stated, "We continue to expect fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $110 million to $125 million, with adjusted net income in the range of $0.40 to $0.95 per diluted share."
  • The outlook reflects ongoing retail margin pressure through the fiscal second quarter, with anticipated improvement in industry inventory levels and gross margins in the low 30% range for the year.
  • Management expects same-store sales to finish fiscal 2026 flat to slightly positive, depending on mix.

Financial Results

  • First quarter revenue was $505 million, driven by nearly 11% same-store sales growth, though unit volume declined by low to mid-single digits, offset by a significant increase in average unit price.
  • Gross profit was $160 million, down from the prior year due to margin pressure during the winter and industry inventory overhang. Gross margins are currently more than 400 basis points below historical norms.
  • Selling, general and administrative expenses rose to over $155 million, but adjusted for transaction-related items, SG&A was about $1.7 million higher year-over-year and down 200 basis points as a percentage of revenue.
  • Reported net loss per share was ($0.36) or ($0.21) on an adjusted basis. Adjusted EBITDA was $15.5 million. Cash on hand was nearly $165 million.
  • The company repurchased approximately 6% of shares, acquired Shelter Bay Marina and retail business in the Keys, and invested in new and expanded operations.

Q&A

  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James & Associates: Asked about margin recovery and discounting. McLamb responded that aggressive promotions are expected to persist through the winter, but "early in the spring is the opportunity for margins to begin to recover from the levels that they're at today, which is historically very, very low."
  • Altobello followed up on inventory targets. McLamb said, "We're trying to end fiscal 2026 with inventory turns above 2x."
  • Sean Wagner, Citigroup: Queried on same-store sales and ASPs. McLamb explained that same-store sales growth was driven by a significant increase in average unit selling prices, particularly due to the Fort Lauderdale Boat Show.
  • Wagner also probed on gross margin mix. McLamb clarified that the mix increase from boat sales, which have lower margins, adversely affected consolidated margins but higher-margin businesses contributed favorably.
  • Eric Wold, Texas Capital Securities: Asked about demand across income groups. McGill noted, "The premium end is performing better overall than the value or entry-level end segments."
  • Anna Glaessgen, B. Riley Securities: Inquired about margin cadence and customer deposits. McLamb indicated similar margin pressure in Q2, with opportunities for margin expansion in the June and September quarters. He also noted that customer deposits are "flat year-over-year," which is positive given the environment.
  • Gerrick Johnson, Seaport Research: Sought details on regional demand trends at boat shows and the impact of news events on customer behavior. McGill and McLamb described "start-stop uncertainty" affecting sales, with positive momentum from recent shows but temporary slowdowns linked to external news.
  • Joseph Nolan, Longbow Research: Asked about gross margin drivers and same-store sales cadence. McLamb attributed margin declines primarily to promotions, not mix, and noted strong same-store sales throughout the quarter, especially in October and at the Fort Lauderdale show.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were mainly focused on margin recovery, inventory management, and demand trends, exhibiting a slightly cautious tone tied to industry headwinds and promotional pressure.
  • Management maintained a balanced and realistic tone, acknowledging challenges but expressing cautious optimism, as McLamb noted, "January trends have been solid, thanks in large part to successful boat shows and the month will finish with positive same-store sales."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained measured, with a slight uptick in optimism regarding early sales trends and inventory progress, while analysts maintained a focus on persistent industry challenges and sought clarity on guidance assumptions and margin outlook.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 is unchanged from the previous quarter, with no revisions to key financial targets or margin expectations.
  • There is an increased emphasis on inventory reduction, with a $170 million year-over-year decrease highlighted this quarter versus a $40 million reduction previously.
  • Management commentary shifted to more frequent references to successful premium boat shows and their positive impact on demand and pricing.
  • Analyst questions continued to focus on gross margin, inventory, and demand, similar to last quarter, but there was more attention to cadence and near-term margin recovery.
  • Management's sentiment is stable and focused on execution, while analysts remain vigilant on risks and the pace of industry normalization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Persistently high promotional activity and cautious consumer behavior are impacting margins and demand.
  • Elevated inventory industry-wide and pressure on less capitalized dealers were cited as ongoing challenges.
  • Management is mitigating risks by prioritizing inventory reduction, maintaining a premium market position, and investing in higher-margin, diversified businesses.
  • Macroeconomic uncertainty and external news events continue to cause "start-stop" buying patterns among customers.

Final Takeaway

MarineMax management highlights stable execution amid an uncertain macro environment, with progress in inventory reduction and positive early signals from premium market segments. The company maintains its full-year guidance for fiscal 2026, emphasizing disciplined management, a strong balance sheet, and ongoing investments in high-margin businesses and operational improvements to support long-term shareholder value as the industry approaches normalization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Interstate anticipates sequential net interest margin gains through 2026 as branch optimization and share repurchases intensify
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Interstate BancSystem, Inc. (FIBK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO James Reuter highlighted significant strategic progress in 2025, stating, "We made meaningful progress to improve core profitability, refocus capital investment and optimize our balance sheet through reorienting our footprint to geographies where we have brand density, strong market share and high potential for growth." He detailed branch divestitures in Arizona, Kansas, and Nebraska, the outsourcing of the consumer credit card product, and the discontinuation of indirect lending originations.
  • Reuter announced, "In August of 2025, we announced a share repurchase authorization and began executing under that plan repurchasing approximately 3.7 million shares through year-end for a total of approximately $118 million...Our Board has approved an incremental $150 million share repurchase authorization bringing the total authorization to $300 million."
  • He commented on credit quality improvement: "Criticized loans decreased by $112.3 million or 9.6% in the fourth quarter and non-performing assets decreased by $47.3 million or 26%. Net charge-offs were elevated in the fourth quarter driven by one larger credit for which we had already set a specific reserve of $11.6 million."
  • Reuter described ongoing branch network optimization, noting, "We closed on the sale of our branches in Arizona and Kansas in the fourth quarter, exiting those states." Additional branch sales and closures will consolidate the company’s footprint from 14 states to 10 contiguous states.
  • He introduced a major organizational redesign: "We are changing the organization from a layered, regional and market structure to a flatter model. Our new State Presidents represent high performers, a majority of which are from within bank and select external talent."
  • CFO David Camera stated, "The company reported net income of $108.8 million or $1.08 per diluted share in the fourth quarter compared to $71.4 million or $0.69 per diluted share in the third quarter." Camera also noted, "Net interest income decreased by $0.4 million compared to the prior quarter or 0.2% to $206.4 million."

Outlook

  • Camera provided guidance that incorporates the impact of ongoing branch sales and closures. "We are including an assumption of low single-digit deposit growth for 2026 with normal seasonality."
  • He added, "Our guidance assumes an assumption of roughly flat to slightly lower total loans for 2026, excluding the continued run-off of our indirect portfolio, which will contribute an additional 1% to 2% in total loan decline."
  • Management expects sequential improvement in net interest margin in 2026, with Camera stating, "We also continue to expect sequential improvement in our net interest margin given the expectation for improving spread between loans and deposits and due to the loan repricing dynamic and continued amortization of lower-yielding investment securities."
  • Camera projected, "Our guidance, as displayed includes an assumption that reported NII is approximately 3% lower in the first quarter than the fourth quarter level of 2025."

Financial Results

  • Net income was $108.8 million or $1.08 per diluted share for the quarter. Net interest income was $206.4 million.
  • Non-interest income rose to $106.6 million, with a $62.7 million gain from the Arizona and Kansas branch divestiture.
  • Non-interest expense was $166.7 million, up $8.8 million from the prior quarter, including $2.3 million in branch closure costs and $4.2 million in severance expenses related to the organization redesign.
  • Net charge-offs increased to $22.1 million, largely due to a single credit, with total provision for credit losses at $7.1 million.
  • Loans decreased by $632.8 million, with deposit balances down $516.7 million, driven by the sale of $641.6 million in deposits. Excluding sold deposits, deposits increased in the quarter.
  • The company returned approximately $138 million of capital to shareholders in the quarter, including $90 million from share repurchases and $48 million in dividends. Tangible book value per share rose 2.9% to $22.40.
  • Common equity Tier 1 capital ratio ended the quarter at 14.38%, up 48 basis points sequentially.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about loan balance declines and production trends. Reuter responded that much of the decline was due to payoffs of criticized loans and intentional runoff, stating, "We did see improved loan production in the month of December and some parts of the footprint."
  • Rulis followed up on margin trajectory. Camera replied, "We still see kind of north of 350 by year-end '26. So really no change there...we still think of it as sequential margin improvement every quarter."
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Questioned reinvestment rates. Camera answered, "On the loan side, new production kind of in the low to mid-6s is kind of on a weighted average basis."
  • Clark asked about buyback pace. Camera replied, "We plan to continue executing. Of course, pace will be dependent on market conditions and things like that."
  • Clark also inquired about criticized loan improvement. Reuter said, "Credit is a living, breathing part of a bank...you can expect us to continue to make good progress."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Questioned expense seasonality. Camera guided to "relatively flat next year," estimating "$159 million to $160 million range each quarter."
  • Motta asked about loan guidance and payoffs. Camera confirmed forecasts include some larger payoffs.
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Asked about criticized loan trends and loan growth optimism. Reuter attributed confidence to the organizational redesign and said, "We are seeing increased competition on rates and terms and things. And I will tell you, we're not going to grow for the sake of growth."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Probed Colorado expansion. Reuter stated current branch network is sufficient but will consider additional locations as growth warrants. He noted, "It will be focused on full relationships...it will be a mix of leading with some loans, but with the eye towards getting deposits and full relationships."
  • Timur Braziler, Wells Fargo: Asked about loan maturities and non-relationship loans. Reuter stated approach is situational and each maturity is an opportunity to expand or retain relationships.
  • Tim Coffey, Janney: Inquired about loan-to-deposit targets. Reuter said, "Long term, I don't really like to set a target...it's north of where we are today."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed cautious optimism but pressed for details on the pace of loan growth, margin improvement, buyback strategy, and criticized loan trends. The tone was neutral to slightly positive, with repeated queries about sustainability and growth catalysts.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing structural improvements, capital returns, and credit quality. During Q&A, management remained measured and occasionally cautious, reiterating discipline and long-term focus, with phrases like "we are optimistic" and "we will continue to exercise discipline."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management sentiment shifted from stabilization to a more proactive, growth-focused outlook. Analyst sentiment remained inquisitive, pressing on execution and guidance details.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter reflected a stronger emphasis on capital returns and branch footprint optimization, with an increased share repurchase authorization and accelerated branch sales and closures.
  • Guidance language shifted to flat or slightly lower loans in 2026, compared to prior commentary on slow production and intentional runoff. Margin improvement expectations remained, but with more explicit sequential improvement targets.
  • Analysts continued to focus on loan production, criticized asset trends, and expense management, echoing themes from Q3 but seeking more clarity on the new organizational model’s impact.
  • Key metrics showed net income and non-interest income growth, driven by divestiture gains, and a further rise in capital ratios. Management confidence increased as credit quality improved and organizational redesign progressed.
  • Strategic priorities evolved toward a streamlined organization, targeted capital allocation, and organic growth in high-potential markets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing competition for loans, both in pricing and terms, as a challenge to organic growth.
  • The company faces headwinds from loan balance declines due to intentional runoff, payoffs of criticized loans, and continued indirect portfolio amortization.
  • Expense pressures persist, with severance and branch closure costs, though management expects stabilization.
  • Credit risk remains dynamic, with management emphasizing continued discipline and situational evaluation each quarter.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of loan growth, margin expansion, and the impact of larger loan payoffs on future performance.

Final Takeaway

First Interstate BancSystem concluded 2025 with a sharpened focus on optimizing its branch footprint, returning capital to shareholders, and strengthening core profitability. Management expects sequential improvement in net interest margin throughout 2026, underpinned by disciplined credit management, a streamlined organizational structure, and targeted investments in growth markets. While loan balances are projected to remain flat or decrease slightly amid ongoing competitive pressures, the company’s commitment to organic growth, expanded share repurchase authorization, and proactive capital management positions it for continued progress into the upcoming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nasdaq outlines launch of 23/5 trading and $100M cross-sell run rate target for 2027 as AI-driven innovation accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nasdaq, Inc. (NDAQ) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Adena Friedman stated that 2025 was an “excellent year for Nasdaq as we delivered strong organic growth and accelerated innovation across our business.” She highlighted surpassing $5 billion in annual net revenue and $4 billion in solutions revenue for the first time, record AUM in the index franchise, and record U.S. equities and options revenues. Friedman indicated, “We delivered industry-leading new listings performance and a record $1.2 trillion in listing transfers, the strongest year ever for our Switch program.”
  • Friedman emphasized the embedment of AI across the business, mentioning the launch of the Agentic AI workforce and early client engagement: “The first Agentic worker we introduced, our Agentic Sanctions analyst, has strong early use among our clients. Continuing the momentum this month, we launched our second worker, the Agentic Enhanced Due Diligence Analyst.” She also announced plans to bring 23/5 trading to the Nasdaq stock market in the second half of 2026, subject to regulatory approval.
  • She highlighted a new partnership with BioCatch to enhance Financial Crime Management technology and indicated a goal to surpass $100 million in run rate revenue from cross-sells by the end of 2027.
  • CFO Sarah Youngwood stated, “We closed 2025 with strong momentum following an excellent year for Nasdaq. We delivered over $5 billion in annual revenue for the first time, reflecting strength across the business and performance that met or exceeded our outlook expectations in every division.”

Outlook

  • Management looks ahead to 2026 with expectations of “accelerating capital markets activity, further supported by recent Fed cuts and a very healthy pipeline of late-stage private companies.” Friedman said, “Based on the current market dynamics, we look forward to an active new issuance year.”
  • Youngwood introduced 2026 non-GAAP operating expense guidance of $2.455 billion to $2.535 billion, reflecting a 7% organic growth rate at the midpoint. She noted, “This reflects a non-GAAP organic growth rate of 7% at the midpoint, which includes the in-year benefit of net synergies actioned under our expanded cost program.”

Financial Results

  • Nasdaq reported net revenue of $5.2 billion for the full year, with solutions revenue of $4.0 billion. Operating income was $2.9 billion, and diluted EPS grew 24%. For the fourth quarter, net revenue was $1.4 billion with solutions revenue of $1.1 billion, and diluted EPS increased 27%.
  • Operating expenses in Q4 were $609 million, operating income was $783 million, and free cash flow for 2025 was $2.2 billion. Nasdaq repurchased 7.2 million shares for $616 million and reduced gross leverage to 2.9x.
  • Financial Technology revenue in Q4 was $498 million, with annual revenue of $1.85 billion, and ARR growth at 12%. Market Services delivered record annual net revenue of $1.2 billion, up 17% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Patrick Moley, Piper Sandler, asked about the impact of expanded options expirations and proliferation of 0DTE trading. Friedman responded, “We are definitely seeing early uptick that's really exciting… giving our clients more opportunity to manage risk in a shorter-dated way... we think that this is a trend that will continue to drive both volumes in the markets, but also participation.”
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair, queried about the drivers behind strong growth in equity options volumes. Friedman explained, “It's actually really a broadening out of the investor base, both in retail for the equities markets and in retail and institutional in the options markets... reflective of a structural shift in terms of the interest that investors have in public equities.”
  • Y. Cho, JPMorgan, asked about the Data and Listings pipeline. Friedman described increased issuer and investor interest, noting, “We did unfortunately have an interruption to that with the government shutdown... But that also -- and then we have a lot of active dialogue with companies, late-stage private companies looking to tap the public markets.”
  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies, inquired about financial crime management outlook. Friedman highlighted variability in professional services revenue due to implementation timing, but expressed optimism: “We will see a little bit more variability quarter-to-quarter in revenues... So with that, I think that kind of the building momentum, we're just so happy we have 9 new clients... we're really excited about both what we've been able to do in '25, but also the pipeline of opportunity in '26.”
  • Elias Abboud, BofA, discussed regulatory flexibility for ATS ownership and M&A opportunities. Friedman indicated interest in a more flexible framework and potential for future involvement in the off-exchange space.
  • Additional questions covered capital allocation strategy, tokenization, proxy modernization, and the potential for prediction markets, with management emphasizing a focus on organic growth, innovation, and regulatory engagement.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was inquisitive and focused, with recurring emphasis on drivers of growth, new initiatives, and market structure changes. Skepticism was not pronounced, but several analysts probed for more detail on revenue momentum, pipeline quality, and implementation risks.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently underscoring “momentum,” “excitement,” and “opportunity,” while acknowledging some variability and implementation timing impacts. Statements such as “we are very pleased with it,” “we are really, really excited,” and “I am more confident than ever in our growth story” reflected high confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment from both management and analysts remained positive, with slightly more emphasis this quarter on execution, innovation, and the expanding opportunity set.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to a more assertive outlook for 2026, highlighting the expectation of “accelerating capital markets activity” and “an active new issuance year.”
  • Strategic focus intensified on AI and digital infrastructure, with explicit updates on Agentic AI workforce rollout and the planned 23/5 trading launch in H2 2026.
  • Analysts continued to focus on growth drivers, pipeline robustness, and the impact of regulatory and technology initiatives.
  • Key metrics such as ARR, solutions revenue, and operating margins all showed sequential improvement, and management’s tone was more optimistic about cross-sell and innovation opportunities.
  • The financial crime management business saw continued momentum, with additional detail provided on revenue variability and enterprise deal implementation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing risks from geopolitical tensions, volatile trading dynamics, and regulatory uncertainty.
  • Youngwood noted, “Looking ahead to 2026, we expect an approximately $9 million year-over-year headwind in each quarter from delistings in the previous year, the impact from new proposed changes to listing standards and the amortization roll-off of prior period initial listing fees.”
  • Variability in financial crime management revenues was highlighted due to enterprise implementation timing and professional services fees.
  • Regulatory changes and technology adoption, particularly in tokenization and digital assets, were discussed as areas requiring close engagement with regulators and infrastructure partners.

Final Takeaway

Nasdaq management underscored a year of record financial and operational achievements, driven by innovation, robust market activity, and targeted cost management. The company enters 2026 with a strong balance sheet, expanding AI-enabled product offerings, and the launch of transformative initiatives such as 23/5 trading. Management remains confident in sustaining growth, deepening client relationships, and capitalizing on new market opportunities, while proactively managing expense growth and navigating a complex regulatory landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Siemens overtakes SAP as Germany’s most valuable company after software selloff
data.description:
Siemens (SIEGY) (SMAWF) has become Germany’s largest company by market capitalization after a sharp selloff in SAP (SAP) (SAPGF) shares knocked the software group from the top spot.

SAP stock dropped as much as 17% on Thursday after the company issued a weaker-than-expected sales outlook, reducing its market value to about €199 billion ($237.8 billion). A quarterly trading update raised investor concerns about how rapidly advancing artificial intelligence tools could disrupt demand across the enterprise software sector.

At the same time, Siemens (SIEGY) (SMAWF) shares climbed as much as 4.6%, lifting the industrial conglomerate’s valuation to roughly €209 billion. Investors have been increasingly optimistic about Siemens’ (SIEGY) (SMAWF) exposure to AI-driven infrastructure spending, particularly through its energy business, which is expected to benefit from rising demand for power and equipment at data centers.

Siemens’ (SIEGY) (SMAWF) automation division is also viewed as well positioned to gain from wider adoption of artificial intelligence. The stock received an additional boost after Swiss rival ABB (ABBNY) (ABBV) projected stronger profitability, reinforcing positive sentiment toward the industrial automation sector.

data.title: Eventbrite to pull, refile HSR merger notification for Bending Spoons
data.description:
Eventbrite (EB) plans to pull and refile its premerger notification for its planned sale to Bending Spoons.

Bending Spoons will withdraw its HSR notification on Jan. 30 and refile on Feb. 3, according to a proxy filing on Wednesday. The waiting period under the HSR Act will then expire no later than March 5. 

Eventbrite (EB) also set its shareholder vote for its sale to Bending Spoons for Feb. 27. 

Eventbrite (EB) continues to expect the sale will be completed in the first half of 2026. 

Ticketing platform Eventbrite (EB) said last month it will be taken private by Italian technology conglomerate Bending Spoons in an all-cash deal valued at about $500 million, or $4.50 a share.

Shares of Eventbrite (EB) ticked lower by 0.8%.

data.title: Modine to divest performance technologies unit to Gentherm for ~$1B
data.description:
Modine Manufacturing (MOD) stock jumped after the thermal management technology company announced a sale of its performance technologies business to Gentherm (THRM) for ~$1B.

The transaction value represents a multiple of ~6.8x LTM (Sep.) 2025 post-synergy adjusted EBITDA of $147M.

MOD shares rose as high as 24.5%, the most intraday since July 2024.

Before the spin-off, Modine is expected to receive a cash distribution of $210M, while the company's stockholders are set to receive ~21M shares of newly issued Gentherm common stock.

Modine shareholders are expected to own ~40% of the combined company, while Gentherm stockholders are expected to own ~60%.

Both boards unanimously approved the transaction.

On a pro forma basis, the combined company had revenue of $2.6B, adjusted EBITDA margin of 13% on a synergy-adjusted basis, and net leverage of ~1.0x.

The combined company is expected to see ~$25M of annual synergies through cost optimization and operational efficiencies.

The transaction is expected to be accretive to adjusted EPS by year two.

Modine Performance Technologies will retain its brand name. Gentherm's board is set to expand by two additional board nominees designated by Modine.

The RMT, or Reverse Morris Trust, transaction is expected to close in the fourth quarter of 2026.

Modine said it will retain its climate solutions businesses, focused on data center and commercial HVAC and refrigeration end markets.

"Overall, we view the transaction as positive, strategically and financially," noted William Blair analyst Brian Drab, noting that the sale of the entire engine business is a "surprise, but not a negative surprise".

data.title: Cimpress raises fiscal 2026 guidance, targets at least $600M EBITDA by 2028 amid robust elevated product growth and operational efficiencies
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cimpress plc (CMPR) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Robert Keane, Founder, Chairman & CEO, highlighted that "elevated products are driving a step function improvement in our per customer lifetime value," specifically noting a 9% year-over-year growth in variable gross profit per customer at reported currency rates. Keane stated that Cimpress has made "significant progress" in optimizing its production footprint, building focused production hubs, and accelerating new product introductions in elevated categories, which have required elevated capital expenditures but are core to the company's competitive advantage.
  • Keane emphasized the expansion of shared technology and organizational delayering, allowing Cimpress to drive efficiencies and reduce operating expenses, citing the deepened collaboration between Vista, National Pen, and BuildASign as a key example. He noted, "we are deepening the collaboration between Vista, National Pen and BuildASign to share product development, sourcing, performance marketing, telesales, direct mail and manufacturing, while maintaining separate focused brands."
  • Keane also expressed increasing confidence in the path to fiscal 2028 EBITDA of at least $600 million, supported by cost efficiencies in goods, technology, and marketing, and indicated a healthy pipeline of tuck-in M&A and partnership opportunities.
  • Sean Quinn, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Q2 marked a milestone for Cimpress. We exceeded $1 billion in quarterly revenue for the first time ever, with organic constant currency growth of 4% through the first half of the year, which was ahead of the annual guidance range that we had previously provided of 2% to 3%." Quinn added, "We are raising our annual guidance for revenue, for adjusted EBITDA and for free cash flow."

Outlook

  • Management raised expectations for fiscal 2026, now forecasting revenue growth of 7% to 8% and 3% to 4% organic constant currency revenue growth. Net income is expected to be at least $79 million, with adjusted EBITDA of at least $460 million, up from $450 million previously. Operating cash flow is projected at approximately $313 million and adjusted free cash flow at approximately $145 million, up from $140 million. Net leverage is expected to decrease slightly by the end of fiscal 2026 from the FY 2025 level of 3.1x.
  • Long-term, Cimpress reiterated confidence in fiscal 2028 targets: 4% to 6% organic constant currency growth, $200 million in net income, adjusted EBITDA of at least $600 million, and free cash flow conversion of approximately 45%.

Financial Results

  • Q2 revenue grew 11% on a reported basis and 4% on an organic constant currency basis, with growth across all segments. Vista saw 5% organic constant currency growth, driven by double-digit gains in promotional products, apparel and gifts, packaging, and labels.
  • Adjusted EBITDA increased by $6.6 million year-over-year, with profit dollars up 8% on a consolidated basis. Gross margins declined by 110 basis points, primarily due to tariff impacts at National Pen. Vista segment EBITDA improved by 10% or approximately $10 million.
  • Adjusted free cash flow for the quarter was an inflow of $124 million, reflecting higher capital expenditures for production expansion and efficiency investments. Net leverage at quarter-end was 2.97x trailing 12-month EBITDA, down sequentially, with $258 million in cash and $250 million undrawn on the credit facility.

Q&A

  • Analyst asked about Vista's holiday season performance and trends in holiday cards. Sean Quinn responded that "Overall, it was a strong quarter for Vista," with North America as the main source of strength. He noted that "volume and holiday cards and calendars in the U.S. was flat year-over-year. And in Canada, it grew double digits."
  • On outperformance vs. initial FY '26 guidance, Quinn explained there were no "big areas of outperformance" but "a solid quarter of execution really across the board," with currency providing additional support.
  • Asked about double-digit growth in Vista's elevated product categories, Robert Keane said, "the strong growth you're talking about really does demonstrate how we've been driving our wallet share of SMBs, thanks to the past and the ongoing investments."
  • Regarding Print Group North America, Keane noted revenues are still small at "about $3 million for the first half, but growing quarter-over-quarter at a fast clip," and emphasized the focus on building out production capabilities.
  • On the collaboration between Vista, National Pen, and BuildASign, Keane explained, "we are going to keep the brands separate and focus on integrating ... back-end capabilities ... to drive growth and drive profitability at VistaPrint in North America."
  • Quinn clarified that $22.6 million allocated for purchase of noncontrolling interest involved two transactions in the PrintBrothers segment, with $6 million of redeemable noncontrolling interest outstanding at the end of the quarter.
  • On tuck-in M&A, Keane described a recent acquisition of an Austrian printing group with $70 million in annual revenues and $5 million in annualized EBITDA prior to synergies, stating the deal "very easily clears the hurdle" for returns and is expected to have "very significant synergy opportunities ahead."
  • Quinn addressed capital allocation, stating, "we were really happy to allocate a little over $25 million to repurchases in Q2 ... we still believe it's a very good use of capital at recent price levels."
  • On agentic commerce, Keane said, "agentic commerce is coming. We are working on that ... we will be, if not the lead baton thrower, but very much at the front of the parade."
  • On the impact of the Jamaica hurricane, Keane said operations are "not back at 100% ... but the teams are back at their desks," and Quinn added, "operationally, we're back to a stable place."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on execution, guidance raises, synergy realization, and capital allocation, with a tone that was neutral to slightly positive, seeking specifics on performance drivers and future plans.
  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was confident, evidenced by statements like "we remain confident in our multiyear plans" and "we are raising our annual guidance." During Q&A, management maintained composure and provided detailed explanations, using language such as "we feel good about where we're at" and "absolutely, yes" when asked about M&A hurdle rates.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment from both analysts and management remained steady, with management slightly more confident due to stronger execution and raised guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 was raised from the previous range, reflecting improved performance in the first half of the year.
  • Strategic focus on elevated products, operational efficiencies, and technology sharing advanced further, with deeper inter-brand collaboration announced this quarter.
  • Analysts continued to probe on guidance, M&A, and cost controls, with management providing more granularity and direct answers than in Q1.
  • Key financial metrics such as adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow showed sequential improvement, and the company achieved its first-ever $1 billion revenue quarter.
  • Management's confidence in achieving FY '28 targets increased, with further clarity on the bridge to those goals.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff impacts, particularly at National Pen, were noted as a headwind, though management expects these to "lessen in future quarters as supply chain remediation continues to ramp up."
  • The hurricane in Jamaica presented operational challenges, but management stated the impact was mitigated and expects to recover some costs through insurance.
  • Higher capital expenditures for manufacturing expansion were discussed, with management viewing them as necessary for long-term competitiveness.
  • Currency fluctuations were cited as both a benefit this quarter and a potential variable for future results.

Final Takeaway

Halfway through fiscal 2026, Cimpress is on track to deliver improved financial results and has raised its annual guidance, reflecting strong execution across product innovation, operational efficiency, and strategic collaboration. The company remains focused on driving elevated product growth, leveraging technology to reduce costs, and pursuing synergistic M&A opportunities, all reinforcing its confidence in achieving the fiscal 2028 targets of at least $600 million in adjusted EBITDA and substantial deleveraging of the balance sheet.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Airbus is said to weigh bigger A220 variant as early sales talks begin
data.description:
Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) is preparing to approach airlines and aircraft lessors about a larger version of its A220 regional jet, Reuters reported Thursday, citing industry sources, with the aim of moving toward a formal development launch later this year.

The effort centers on securing enough preliminary commitments to support a stretched A220-500 model that could seat around 180 passengers. The outreach marks the beginning of more substantive commercial discussions ahead of a possible launch decision, which could come as soon as the Farnborough Airshow in July.

Executives from Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) told financiers during the Airlines Economics conference in Dublin that 2026 is expected to be a pivotal year for the A220 program, adding that additional details would be shared in the near future.

Any move to proceed with development of the proposed aircraft, described by industry observers as a relatively straightforward stretch of the existing design, would still require approval from the Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) board, Reuters reported.
